title: Insulating a hot water cylinder
subtitle: Fitting a cylinder jacket and lagging the pipework
excerpt: An uninsulated hot-water cylinder loses around 1,400 kWh per year in standing heat losses. A correctly fitted cylinder jacket and lagged pipes reduce that to under 100 kWh, saving roughly 90 pounds per year at current gas rates.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust hot-water cylinder guidance for standing-loss figures. BS EN 12897 for UK cylinder labelling standards. gov.uk Part G for hot-water system requirements.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-formed insulation wrap fitted around a hot-water cylinder to reduce standing heat losses. UK standard jackets are 80 mm thick and reduce standing losses by around 70-75 percent compared to a bare cylinder.

content.text: A bare copper hot-water cylinder loses heat continuously whether the boiler is running or not. An uninsulated 120-litre cylinder loses around 1,400 kWh per year in standing losses alone. At 2025/26 gas cap rates of 6p/kWh, that is roughly 84 pounds per year wasted. A standard 80 mm
cylinder jacket

reduces standing losses to around 200 kWh per year. Combined with lagging the first metre of hot pipe from the cylinder, the saving exceeds 1,200 kWh per year. The payback period is typically under six months.
What you need
Materials

Cylinder jacket, 80 mm thick, sized to match your cylinder diameter and height. Measure the cylinder: diameter and height in mm. Most UK domestic cylinders are 400-450 mm diameter and 900-1050 mm tall; pre-cut jacket sizes cover these standard dimensions.
Foam pipe lagging: 22 mm bore for standard hot-water pipe, 28 mm bore for the primary flow pipe from the boiler. Buy enough to cover the first 1 m of every hot pipe leaving the cylinder.
Duct tape or pipe-lagging tape: to seal the lagging joints and the jacket overlap seam.
Do not insulate the underside of the cylinder or the thermostat access point. Do not cover the immersion heater boss.
The installation
Measure the cylinder. Note the diameter at the widest point and the overall height from base to dome. Note the positions of the immersion heater boss and the thermostat pocket.

Open the jacket flat. The jacket is supplied as a flat-pack assembly of foam segments held together with fabric ties. Lay it out on the floor to identify the base panel, side segments, and dome panel.
Fit the base panel. Slide it under the cylinder if the cylinder is raised on legs. If the cylinder sits directly on a board, wrap the base panel around the lower half instead.

Wrap the side segments around the cylinder. Work upward from the base, overlapping each segment by at least 20 mm. Tie the segments to the cylinder using the fabric ties at mid-height and near the top.
Fit the dome panel over the top, pushing it down to meet the side segments. Secure with duct tape along the seam if the ties do not draw tight enough.

Cut and fit the pipe lagging. Measure the hot pipe from the top of the cylinder outward. Cut the foam lagging with a utility knife. Slit the lagging lengthways if it is not pre-slit. Snap it over the pipe and tape the cut joint every 200 mm.

Check the thermostat access pocket is not blocked. You need to be able to read and adjust the thermostat through a gap in the jacket. Cut a small window in the jacket foam if the existing pocket flap does not align with the thermostat position.
Commissioning

Run the cylinder through one full heating cycle. Check that the hot tap reaches at least 60 degrees C within an hour of the heating turning off. If recovery time has grown longer, check that the thermostat pocket is clear and the bulb is touching the cylinder wall.
What the numbers look like

A cylinder maintained at 60 degrees C in an airing cupboard at 18 degrees C loses heat at a rate that depends on the total insulation thickness. A bare cylinder (typically 25 mm factory foam) loses around 1.5 kWh per day in standing losses. An 80 mm jacket reduces that to around 0.5 kWh per day. Over a year the saving is approximately 365 kWh. At 2025/26 gas cap rates, that is around 22 pounds per year from the jacket alone. Pipe lagging adds a further 20-40 pounds per year depending on the length and temperature of the pipework.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Hot water takes longer to heat than before fitting the jacket
attrs.cause: Thermostat bulb is no longer in contact with the cylinder wall
attrs.fix: Open the thermostat access pocket and check the bulb is seated in its pocket or taped against the cylinder wall. If needed, cut a larger access window in the jacket.

attrs.cause: Ties are loose or the side segments are not overlapping enough
attrs.fix: Re-tie the fabric straps at mid-height and again at the top of the side segments. Add a duct tape band around the outside of the jacket at the tie positions.

attrs.symptom: Pipes sweat or drip after lagging
attrs.cause: Cold pipe inside the airing cupboard is condensing ambient moisture
attrs.fix: Cold pipes in warm spaces sweat when uninsulated. Lag cold-water pipes in the airing cupboard with 13 mm foam lagging to stop condensation on the pipe surface.
